marks.label: Five tapering arms
marks.description: The classic orange star: five stout arms tapering to points. Turns up almost anywhere, often near mussel beds.

marks.label: Pale spine line down each arm
marks.description: A single pale line of low, knobbly spines runs down the middle of each arm - no big spike rows like the spiny starfish.

perMark.label: Five tapering arms
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and encompasses almost the entire starfish rather than highlighting the overall body shape cleanly.

perMark.label: Pale spine line down each arm
perMark.note: The circle is offset to the left, capturing only half of one arm and mostly empty background water.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and clearly shows the starfish features, but the circles are poorly sized and positioned. Circle 1 should be resized to neatly frame the entire starfish, and Circle 2 should be shrunk and centered directly over the pale spine line of a single arm.

marks.label: Five short stubby arms
marks.description: Five short, fat arms rather than long points - giving a plump, cushiony feel.

marks.label: Pentagon outline
marks.description: The whole animal is a small (to 5 cm) greenish, knobbly pentagon, not a pointed star. Found under stones on the lower shore.

perMark.label: Five short stubby arms
perMark.note: The circle is centered on the central disc rather than highlighting the individual stubby arms.

perMark.label: Pentagon outline
perMark.note: The circle is slightly too small and off-center to perfectly trace the pentagonal outline of the animal.

validation.summary: The photo is clear and shows the cushion star well, but the concentric circles are poorly aligned. Circle 1 should be adjusted to point directly to one of the short arms, and Circle 2 should be resized to encompass the entire pentagonal body outline.

marks.label: Four gonad rings
marks.description: The four pale horseshoe loops on top of the bell are the gonads. They are the single most reliable moon-jelly tell; no other UK jelly shows this four-ring clover.

marks.label: Four frilly oral arms
marks.description: Four ribbon-like mouth-arms hang from the centre of the bell. They are short and frilly, not the long stinging tentacles of a lion's mane.

marks.label: Short tentacle fringe
marks.description: Only a fine fringe of very short tentacles rings the bell margin (vs the dense trailing mane of Cyanea). Harmless to touch.

perMark.label: Four gonad rings
perMark.note: The circle is well-centered on the pale horseshoe-shaped gonad rings visible through the translucent bell.

perMark.label: Four frilly oral arms
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the central, ribbon-like oral arms hanging down from the bell.

perMark.label: Short tentacle fringe
perMark.note: The circle perfectly targets the fine, short fringe of tentacles lining the margin of the bell.

validation.summary: The photo is exceptionally clear with a stark black background, and all three diagnostic features are perfectly aligned and highly visible.

marks.label: Tall first dorsal (male)
marks.description: Breeding males raise a very tall, sail-like first dorsal fin (females and resting fish keep it folded flat).

marks.label: Flat-bottomed, sits on sand
marks.description: A flattened body that lies pressed to the sand, with eyes set high on top of the head — a scooter, not a free-swimmer.

marks.label: Blue stripes & spots (male)
marks.description: Electric-blue wavy stripes and spots across the head and body of the male; females are plain mottled brown.

perMark.label: Tall first dorsal (male)
perMark.note: The tall first dorsal fin spine is clearly visible inside the circle, though the fin is folded down.

perMark.label: Flat-bottomed, sits on sand
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the pectoral fin and body while the fish is being held in a hand, not sitting on sand.

perMark.label: Blue stripes & spots (male)
perMark.note: The circle is on the head showing the blue markings, but it is labeled as 3 while the blue stripes are also prominent across the rest of the body.

validation.summary: The photo shows a dead or captured fish being held in a hand out of water, which fails to demonstrate its natural flat-bottomed posture on sand. It should be replaced with an underwater photo of a live dragonet in its natural habitat.

marks.label: Sharp cone, straight sides
marks.description: A glossy, sharply conical shell with straight sides and a flat base, like a smooth spinning top. Pinkish flecks over 10-12 whorls.

marks.label: Pointed apex, no navel
marks.description: Comes to a fine point at the top, and (unlike the flat top shell) has no open navel hole on the underside.

perMark.label: Sharp cone, straight sides
perMark.note: The large circle perfectly highlights the straight, flat-sided conical whorls with pinkish flecks.

perMark.label: Pointed apex, no navel
perMark.note: The circle is well-placed on the pointed apex, though the lack of an umbilical opening cannot be seen from this angle.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and the circles are accurately placed to show the straight-sided cone shape and the pointed apex.

marks.label: Pie-crust shell edge
marks.description: The front margin of the broad oval shell is crimped like the edge of a pie - unmistakable once you've seen it.

marks.label: Broad oval shell
marks.description: A wide, smooth oval shell (reddish-brown in life) with big black-tipped pincers. The biggest crab you'll meet on the shore.

marks.label: Pie-crust shell edge
marks.description: The front edge of the broad reddish shell is crimped like the crust of a pie - the classic edible (brown) crab mark.

marks.label: Black-tipped claws
marks.description: Both heavy pincers end in black tips.

perMark.label: Pie-crust shell edge
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the underside of the crab carapace, which is upside down and missing its legs.

perMark.label: Broad oval shell
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the underside of the crab shell, not showing the broad dorsal oval shape.

perMark.label: Pie-crust shell edge
perMark.note: The circle is on the underside of the crab and does not show the characteristic pie-crust edge.

perMark.label: Black-tipped claws
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the rocky background where no claws are present.

validation.summary: The photo shows an upside-down, dead crab shell missing its legs and claws, making it impossible to see the diagnostic features. Replace this photo with a live specimen showing its dorsal side and claws clearly.

marks.label: Five teeth each side
marks.description: Count five sharp teeth along each side of the shell, behind the eyes (plus three blunt bumps between the eyes). The classic shore-crab tell.

marks.label: Pointed walking legs
marks.description: The legs end in simple points, never flattened into swimming paddles - that rules out the velvet and harbour crabs.

perMark.label: Five teeth each side
perMark.note: The circle is well-centered on the carapace edge showing the distinct teeth clearly.

perMark.label: Pointed walking legs
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the pointed tip of the walking leg against the seabed.

validation.summary: The photo is clear and the circles are well-placed to show the diagnostic teeth on the carapace and the pointed walking legs.

marks.label: Thick upper lip
marks.description: The upper lip is very thick (nearly as deep as the eye diameter) and warty-looking — the give-away vs thin-lipped and golden-grey mullet.

marks.label: Grey, striped flanks
marks.description: Plain grey-silver body with faint horizontal dark streaks along each scale row; a robust, deep-bodied mullet.

marks.label: Two separated dorsal fins
marks.description: A short spiny first dorsal followed by a longer soft second fin, well separated as in all grey mullet.

perMark.label: Thick upper lip
perMark.note: The circle is placed completely off the fish on the rocky background to the right.

perMark.label: Grey, striped flanks
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far forward and high, though some striped flank is visible; the largest unnumbered circle actually covers the flanks better.

perMark.label: Two separated dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle only covers the first spiny dorsal fin and does not show the separation or the second fin.

validation.summary: The circles are misaligned, with circle 1 placed completely off the fish on the background. Reposition circle 1 onto the thick upper lip, use the large central circle for the striped flanks, and adjust circle 3 to show the separation between the two dorsal fins.

marks.label: Radiating compass marks
marks.description: Dark brown V-shapes radiate from the centre of the bell like the points of a compass - the single best tell for this species.

marks.label: Dark central ring
marks.description: A small dark ring sits at the very top of the bell, where all the V-marks meet.

marks.label: Long trailing tentacles
marks.description: Twenty-four long, fine tentacles trail from the bell margin, plus four frilly mouth-arms. It stings.

perMark.label: Radiating compass marks
perMark.note: The radiating V-shaped brown lines are exceptionally clear and well-framed by this large circle.

perMark.label: Dark central ring
perMark.note: The circle is offset too high, leaving the actual dark central ring at the bottom edge of the circle.

perMark.label: Long trailing tentacles
perMark.note: The circle is placed over dark water and seaweed, barely catching a few faint, thin tentacle strands.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent, but circle 2 needs to be centered directly on the dark central ring, and circle 3 should be moved higher up to capture the thicker, more visible tentacles and mouth-arms just below the bell.

marks.label: Long scaleless grey body
marks.description: A heavy, scaleless, grey-brown eel body (the true eel is yellow-brown and freshwater). Hides in wreck and rock holes by day.

marks.label: Dorsal fin starts forward
marks.description: The long dorsal fin begins just behind the pectoral fin and runs to the tail — much further forward than in the common eel.

marks.label: Big head, wide gape
marks.description: A large head with a wide mouth and stout jaws; it does have pectoral fins (a moray has none).

perMark.label: Long scaleless grey body
perMark.note: The large circle correctly encompasses a major portion of the long grey body.

perMark.label: Dorsal fin starts forward
perMark.note: The circle is placed far too far back on the mid-body instead of near the pectoral fin origin.

perMark.label: Big head, wide gape
perMark.note: The circle is well-centered on the head, showing the mouth and eye clearly.

validation.summary: The overall photo is clear and shows the eel well, but circle 2 needs to be moved forward to where the dorsal fin actually begins near the pectoral fin.

marks.label: Reddish-brown bell
marks.description: A broad, domed bell in warm reddish-brown - the UK's largest jelly. Cooler-coloured blue jelly (C. lamarckii) is the smaller lookalike.

marks.label: Dense mane of tentacles
marks.description: Hundreds of long, fine tentacles hang in a thick 'mane' beneath the bell (vs the short fringe of a moon jelly). Painful sting.

marks.label: Orange domed bell
marks.description: A solid orange-tan domed bell.

marks.label: Mane of fine tentacles
marks.description: A dense trailing mass of many fine, hair-like tentacles - the 'lion's mane'.

perMark.label: Reddish-brown bell
perMark.note: The circle is very large and shifted to the left, capturing mostly water and only a portion of the reddish bell.

perMark.label: Dense mane of tentacles
perMark.note: The circle is placed over the central bell area rather than the trailing tentacles.

perMark.label: Orange domed bell
perMark.note: The circle is placed entirely off the jellyfish on the sandy seabed to the left.

perMark.label: Mane of fine tentacles
perMark.note: The circle covers a large portion of the bell and some tentacles, but is too large and poorly targeted.

validation.summary: The current circles are extremely large, overlapping, and mostly misaligned, with one circle completely off the animal. They need to be resized and repositioned to accurately target the bell and the tentacles of the jellyfish.

marks.label: Blue-purple bell
marks.description: Vivid blue to purple, the colour deepening towards the centre of the bell. Like a small lion's mane but cool-coloured, not reddish-brown.

marks.label: Radiating canals
marks.description: Dark lines radiate from the centre, with a mass of fine trailing tentacles beneath. Mild sting.

marks.label: Blue-tinted bell
marks.description: A domed bell with a distinctive blue-violet tint and radial markings.

marks.label: Fine trailing tentacles
marks.description: A fringe of many fine tentacles; smaller and bluer than the orange lion's mane.

perMark.label: Blue-purple bell
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and encompasses almost the entire jellyfish rather than focusing on the blue-purple bell coloration.

perMark.label: Radiating canals
perMark.note: The circle is centered on the middle of the bell rather than highlighting the radiating canals specifically.

perMark.label: Blue-tinted bell
perMark.note: This very large circle overlaps with circle 1 and covers almost the entire organism instead of targeting the outer bell.

perMark.label: Fine trailing tentacles
perMark.note: The circle is placed mostly on the sandy beach background where no tentacles are visible.

validation.summary: The current circles are nested, oversized, and poorly targeted, with circle 4 completely off the animal on the sand. The marks need to be completely re-authored with smaller, non-overlapping circles that point to the specific anatomical features.

marks.label: Two separate dorsal fins
marks.description: A spiny first dorsal fin and a longer soft second dorsal fin, clearly separated — a perch-family hallmark.

marks.label: Silver-grey flanks, dark back
marks.description: Plain silver-grey flanks scaling up to a darker back; no bars or obvious markings in adults.

marks.label: Two dorsal fins (front spiny)
marks.description: Two separate dorsal fins; the front one is spiny. Clean silver body with no spots.

marks.label: Spiny gill cover
marks.description: Two flat spines on the rear edge of the gill cover (preopercle) — a prick-check confirms it quickly.

marks.label: Spiny gill cover
marks.description: A sharp spine on the rear edge of the gill cover (operculum) - mind it when handling.

marks.label: Pointed head, large mouth
marks.description: Pointed head with a large terminal mouth (vs the blunt, thick-lipped grey mullet).

perMark.label: Two separate dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle is floating in the sky far above the fish.

perMark.label: Silver-grey flanks, dark back
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the spiny dorsal fin rather than the flanks.

perMark.label: Two dorsal fins (front spiny)
perMark.note: The circle is floating high in the sky above the fish.

perMark.label: Spiny gill cover
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the mid-body scales instead of the gill cover.

perMark.label: Spiny gill cover
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the mid-body scales instead of the gill cover.

perMark.label: Pointed head, large mouth
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the second dorsal fin and background instead of the head.

validation.summary: The circles are completely misaligned, with several floating in the sky or placed on the wrong parts of the fish. All markers need to be repositioned to their correct anatomical features.

marks.label: Eight arms, no fins
marks.description: Soft, bulbous body and eight long arms, no fins. Orange-brown; our commonest octopus, on rocky and shelly bottoms.

perMark.label: Eight arms, no fins
perMark.note: The circle is placed over the water surface showing only a few arms, while the main body of the octopus is outside the circle.

validation.summary: The circle is off-center and misses the main body of the octopus. Reposition the circle to encompass the entire octopus to clearly show the body and arms.

marks.label: Pale band on first dorsal fin
marks.description: A pale orange/yellow band runs along the top edge of the first dorsal fin - the rock goby's tell.

marks.label: Robust dark body
marks.description: A chunky, dark, mottled goby (to ~12 cm), much bigger and darker than the little sand/common gobies. Rocky shores.

marks.label: Pale-edged first dorsal
marks.description: A pale or orange band along the top edge of the front dorsal fin - the rock goby's clearest mark.

marks.label: Blunt head, thick lips
marks.description: A broad blunt head with thick lips, eyes set high.

marks.label: Two dorsal fins
marks.description: Two separate dorsal fins along the back (a goby trait); body stocky and mottled dark.

marks.label: Blunt head, belly sucker
marks.description: Stocky blunt head; the pelvic fins are fused into a round sucker on the belly (shared by all gobies).

perMark.label: Pale band on first dorsal fin
perMark.note: The circle is too large and shifted slightly low, but the pale band on the first dorsal fin is visible.

perMark.label: Robust dark body
perMark.note: The circle is placed entirely on the blurry background rocks to the right of the fish.

perMark.label: Pale-edged first dorsal
perMark.note: This circle is placed over the second dorsal fin and tail region, not the first dorsal fin.

perMark.label: Blunt head, thick lips
perMark.note: The circle is shifted too far forward into the background, only catching the very tip of the snout.

perMark.label: Two dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and mostly covers empty background space above the fish.

perMark.label: Blunt head, belly sucker
perMark.note: The circle is placed far below the fish on the blurry background rocks.

validation.summary: Several circles are misaligned or floating in the background. Reposition and resize the circles to accurately target the fish's features, and remove duplicate markers for the dorsal fin band.

marks.label: Long spindly legs
marks.description: Long, thin, spider-like legs - far longer relative to the body than any of the true crabs here.

marks.label: Teardrop shell, pointed snout
marks.description: The shell is teardrop-shaped, narrowing to a forked point at the front, and is often coated in seaweed and hydroids.

marks.label: Triangular pear-shaped shell
marks.description: The shell narrows to a point at the front, giving a triangular, pear-shaped outline.

marks.label: Long spindly legs
marks.description: Long, thin spider-like legs, often draped in weed or debris as camouflage.

perMark.label: Long spindly legs
perMark.note: The circle is placed over the legs but is overlapping multiple limbs and the body, making it confusing.

perMark.label: Teardrop shell, pointed snout
perMark.note: The circle is placed entirely in the empty water background above the crab.

perMark.label: Triangular pear-shaped shell
perMark.note: The circle is positioned on the empty background space next to the crab's body.

perMark.label: Long spindly legs
perMark.note: The circle is placed in the empty background space far above the actual legs.

validation.summary: Most of the circles are misaligned and floating in the empty background space above the crab. They need to be repositioned to accurately target the crab's shell and legs.

marks.label: Electric blue head & stripes
marks.description: The breeding male is unmistakable: brilliant blue head and wavy blue stripes streaking back over an orange body.

marks.label: Orange flanks
marks.description: Bright orange flanks under the blue lines (males). A slim, pointed-headed wrasse.

perMark.label: Electric blue head & stripes
perMark.note: The circle perfectly captures the brilliant blue head and facial patterns of the male.

perMark.label: Orange flanks
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far forward and high, capturing mostly the blue dorsal area rather than centering on the orange flanks below.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and clearly shows the male coloration. Circle 2 should be shifted slightly down and back to center directly on the bright orange flank.

marks.label: Flattened swimming paddles
marks.description: The last pair of legs end in flat, paddle-shaped segments (tips often tinged violet) for swimming - shared with the velvet crab, absent in the shore crab.

marks.label: Five side teeth
marks.description: Five teeth down each side of the shell behind the eyes; reddish overall. A small swimming crab of muddy sand.

marks.label: Paddle back legs
marks.description: The last pair of legs are flattened into swimming paddles - it is a swimming crab.

marks.label: Broad reddish shell
marks.description: A broad reddish-orange shell with the eyes set at the front.

perMark.label: Flattened swimming paddles
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the flattened paddle-shaped segment of the rear leg.

perMark.label: Five side teeth
perMark.note: The circle is centered too far forward on the eyes and front margin rather than the lateral teeth.

perMark.label: Paddle back legs
perMark.note: The circle is placed completely off the crab on the wooden decking.

perMark.label: Broad reddish shell
perMark.note: The circle is well-centered on the main carapace of the crab.

validation.summary: Circle 3 is completely off the animal and needs to be moved to the other paddle leg on the right side. Circle 2 should be adjusted slightly to better highlight the lateral teeth on the side of the shell.

marks.label: No head tentacles
marks.description: The crown is smooth - NO branched tentacles above the eyes (that rules in the shanny vs the tompot blenny).

marks.label: Thick lips, big high-set eyes
marks.description: A blunt, scaleless head with thick lips and large eyes set high and forward - a true blenny.

marks.label: Mottled green-brown body
marks.description: A smooth, scaleless, mottled green-brown body that wedges into crevices and rock pools.

perMark.label: No head tentacles
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the smooth crown above the eye showing no tentacles.

perMark.label: Thick lips, big high-set eyes
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the cheek behind the mouth and eye, completely missing both features.

perMark.label: Mottled green-brown body
perMark.note: The circle is very large and centered on the pectoral fin rather than the main body flank.

validation.summary: Move circle 2 forward to cover the eye and thick lips, and shrink circle 3 to focus directly on the mottled skin of the body flank rather than the pectoral fin.

marks.label: Torpedo body
marks.description: A slender, streamlined torpedo body - the classic squid shape, schooling in midwater. The main squid of UK fisheries.

marks.label: Long rear fins
marks.description: Two triangular fins at the tail run about 70% of the body length (longer than the European squid's, which reach only about half).

perMark.label: Torpedo body
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the mid-body but is too small to encompass the overall torpedo shape of the squid.

perMark.label: Long rear fins
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far forward on the body, missing the full length of the rear fins extending to the tail.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and very clear, but the circles need to be repositioned. Circle 1 should be widened to show the overall torpedo body shape, and Circle 2 should be moved further back toward the tail to properly highlight the long rear fins.

marks.label: Triangular tail fins
marks.description: Two triangular fins at the tail, together reaching a bit over half the body length (shorter than the veined squid's ~70%).

marks.label: Big eye + ten arms
marks.description: A large eye and ten arms (two of them long, retractable catching tentacles). Reddish, fast, schooling over the open shelf.

perMark.label: Triangular tail fins
perMark.note: The circle correctly encompasses the triangular fin on the upper side of the mantle.

perMark.label: Big eye + ten arms
perMark.note: The circle perfectly captures the large blue-ringed eye and the splayed arms.

validation.summary: The photo is an excellent, clear lateral shot of the squid with both diagnostic features perfectly highlighted by the circles.

marks.label: Long arms, rows of spines
marks.description: Five long, tapering arms carrying three rows of conspicuous spines (each spine ringed blue at its base). Can reach 70 cm across.

marks.label: Conspicuous spines
marks.description: The prominent, evenly-spaced white spines separate it from the smoother common starfish.

perMark.label: Long arms, rows of spines
perMark.note: The circle is very small and placed near the central disc, while the large teal ring below it highlights the arm and spines but lacks a number.

perMark.label: Conspicuous spines
perMark.note: The small numbered circle is placed on a piece of shell in the background, while the large unnumbered ring highlights the spines on the arm.

validation.summary: The numbered labels are mismatched and placed on the wrong circles. The large teal rings correctly highlight the arms and spines, but the numbered dots are detached and misaligned.

marks.label: Bright red eyes
marks.description: Vivid red eyes are the instant giveaway - no other common UK crab has them.

marks.label: Velvety carapace
marks.description: The shell is coated in fine velvety fur, dull brown. The last legs (not shown here) are flattened swimming paddles.

marks.label: Red eyes
marks.description: Bright red eyes - unmistakable on this otherwise drab crab.

marks.label: Velvety furred shell
marks.description: The shell is covered in fine velvety fur; the last pair of legs are flattened into blue-lined swimming paddles.

perMark.label: Bright red eyes
perMark.note: The bright red eye is perfectly centered and highly visible.

perMark.label: Velvety carapace
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the fine velvety texture on the upper shell.

perMark.label: Red eyes
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the mouthparts and front edge of the shell instead of the other red eye.

perMark.label: Velvety furred shell
perMark.note: This circle overlaps the mouthparts and antennae area rather than the main carapace.

validation.summary: The red eye on the left is completely unlabelled, while marks 3 and 4 are misaligned over the mouthparts. Move mark 3 to the left red eye and consolidate or remove the redundant velvety shell marks.

marks.label: Pointed spire
marks.description: A short, sharply pointed spire at the top of a thick, solid shell.

marks.label: Short pointed spire
marks.description: A thick, short-spired shell with spiral ridges and an oval mouth drawn out into a little spout. Drills holes in barnacles and mussels.

marks.label: Colour varies with diet
marks.description: The thick body whorl is often boldly banded; colour varies (white to brown/black) with the prey it eats.

marks.label: Colour varies with diet
marks.description: White, grey, banded brown or even orange - the colour follows what it has been eating, so a cluster looks like a mixed bag.

perMark.label: Pointed spire
perMark.note: Mark 1 is completely missing from the image.

perMark.label: Short pointed spire
perMark.note: The circle is too large and mostly covers the blurry background instead of focusing tightly on the spire.

perMark.label: Colour varies with diet
perMark.note: The circle is well placed on the distinct black and white bands of the shell.

perMark.label: Colour varies with diet
perMark.note: This circle overlaps heavily with mark 3 and is redundant on the same individual shell.

validation.summary: Remove the redundant mark 4 and the missing mark 1. Shrink and reposition mark 2 to focus precisely on the pointed spire of the shell.

marks.label: Bulbous, warty body
marks.description: A large bulbous head and eight arms with warty, mottled skin that changes colour in a blink. Dens in rock holes; mostly southern and western in the UK.

perMark.label: Bulbous, warty body
perMark.note: The circle is well-centered on the bulbous mantle showing the mottled, warty skin texture.

validation.summary: The annotation is well-placed on the octopus's mantle, clearly demonstrating the bulbous shape and warty skin texture.

marks.label: Long thin spiny arms
marks.description: Five long, thread-thin arms fringed with fine spines - far more delicate than a true starfish's arms, and they snap off at a touch.

marks.label: Small disc, dense beds
marks.description: A small round central disc sits at the centre of the arms. Hugely variable in colour; often carpets the seabed in writhing beds like this.

perMark.label: Long thin spiny arms
perMark.note: The circle clearly highlights the spiny arms, though the background is somewhat cluttered.

perMark.label: Small disc, dense beds
perMark.note: The circle is well-centered on the small central disc of the brittlestar.

validation.summary: The annotations are correctly placed on the key features of the brittlestar. The image is somewhat busy but clearly shows the diagnostic spiny arms and central disc.

marks.label: Lives in a borrowed shell
marks.description: Not a true crab: a soft-bodied animal living inside an empty snail shell (a periwinkle when small, a whelk when bigger). The shell is the giveaway.

marks.label: Legs and claws poke out
marks.description: Only the legs and unequal claws show, the right claw the larger - used to block the shell entrance when threatened.

perMark.label: Lives in a borrowed shell
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the textured snail shell that the hermit crab is inhabiting.

perMark.label: Legs and claws poke out
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the prominent legs and claws emerging from the shell opening.

validation.summary: The photo is clear and sharp, and both circles are accurately placed to show the borrowed shell and the emerging legs.

marks.label: Radiating ridges
marks.description: A low cone of grey shell with ridges radiating from the peak. Steeper-coned high on the shore, flatter lower down.

marks.label: Clamped to bare rock
marks.description: No spire and no pattern - just a plain cone clamped tight to the rock, grazing algae and homing to the same scar.

perMark.label: Radiating ridges
perMark.note: The circle is placed off-center on the left side of the shell, but the radiating ridges are clearly visible across the entire shell surface.

perMark.label: Clamped to bare rock
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the apex of the shell rather than highlighting the margin where the shell clamps tightly to the bare rock.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and shows the features clearly, but the circles need repositioning. Circle 1 should encompass the whole shell or be centered on it, and Circle 2 should be moved to the edge of the shell where it meets the bare rock substrate.

marks.label: Warty stinging bell
marks.description: The mauve-purple bell is peppered all over with dark stinging warts - unique among the common UK jellies. Glows blue at night.

marks.label: Frilly oral arms
marks.description: Four long, frilly mouth-arms hang from the centre, between the bell and the tentacles.

marks.label: Long trailing tentacles
marks.description: Eight long, slender tentacles trail well below the bell. A notable stinger; fully oceanic, arrives in swarms.

perMark.label: Warty stinging bell
perMark.note: The circle perfectly frames the dome of the bell showing the dark stinging warts clearly.

perMark.label: Frilly oral arms
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the long, spotted oral arms hanging from the center.

perMark.label: Long trailing tentacles
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the thin, trailing tentacles extending below the body.

validation.summary: The photo is exceptionally clear and all three diagnostic features are perfectly aligned and easy to identify. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Row of dark eye-spots
marks.description: A line of white-ringed black eye-spots runs along the base of the dorsal fin - the unmistakable butterfish mark.

marks.label: Long ribbon body
marks.description: A long, flattened, ribbon-like body, slippery to hold (hence butterfish/gunnel). Drapes over rocks.

marks.label: Dorsal eye-spot row
marks.description: A row of dark, white-ringed eye-spots along the base of the long dorsal fin - unmistakable.

marks.label: Tiny pointed head
marks.description: A small, blunt head on the slender ribbon - not eel-like teeth, just a gentle weed-grazer.

marks.label: Long ribbon body
marks.description: A long, strongly side-flattened ribbon body with one continuous low dorsal fin running its length.

marks.label: Small blunt head
marks.description: Small blunt head; the fish is slippery and eel-like, hence the name butterfish (or gunnel).

perMark.label: Row of dark eye-spots
perMark.note: The circle is placed too low on the body flank instead of centering on the dorsal fin base where the spots are.

perMark.label: Long ribbon body
perMark.note: The circle is placed in empty space near the tail fin rather than on the body.

perMark.label: Dorsal eye-spot row
perMark.note: The circle is placed in empty space above the fish, only catching a pectoral fin.

perMark.label: Tiny pointed head
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the small, blunt head of the fish.

perMark.label: Long ribbon body
perMark.note: The circle is well placed on the main section of the ribbon-like body.

perMark.label: Small blunt head
perMark.note: The circle is placed near the tail end of the fish instead of the head.

validation.summary: Several circles are misaligned, with some floating in empty space or placed on the wrong end of the fish. Reposition the markers to correctly target the head, body, and dorsal eye-spots.

marks.label: Kinked lateral line
marks.description: The dark line down the flank bends sharply upward over the pectoral fin — pollack's most reliable single ID against bib and cod.

marks.label: Projecting lower jaw
marks.description: Lower jaw juts out beyond the upper, giving a slight under-bite silhouette. Cod and bib have an even or upper-jawed bite.

marks.label: No chin barbel
marks.description: Smooth chin — no whisker. This rules out cod and bib at a glance; both gadoids have a clear chin barbel.

perMark.label: Kinked lateral line
perMark.note: The circle is too large and centered too far back, missing the sharpest part of the upward curve over the pectoral fin.

perMark.label: Projecting lower jaw
perMark.note: The circle is floating in the open water above the fish's head, completely missing the jaw.

perMark.label: No chin barbel
perMark.note: The circle is placed over the eye and cheek area instead of the chin.

validation.summary: The photo shows a clear profile of a pollack, but the circles are badly misaligned. Move circle 1 forward to highlight the curve of the lateral line, move circle 2 to the tip of the snout and lower jaw, and move circle 3 to the smooth chin.

marks.label: Small mottled sandy goby
marks.description: A tiny (to ~6 cm) sandy-mottled goby. Very like the sand goby; common goby favours brackish estuaries and pools.

marks.label: Pelvic-fin sucker
marks.description: Like all gobies, the pelvic fins are fused into a little sucker disc it uses to perch on the bottom.

marks.label: Perched, rounded head
marks.description: Rests propped on the bottom with a blunt rounded head and large eyes set high.

perMark.label: Small mottled sandy goby
perMark.note: The circle is centered on the pectoral fin and mid-body rather than encompassing the whole fish to show its small mottled form.

perMark.label: Pelvic-fin sucker
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the rocky substrate in front of the fish where no pelvic fin is present.

perMark.label: Perched, rounded head
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far forward on the snout, partially missing the high-set eyes.

validation.summary: The circles are misaligned. Circle 2 needs to be moved to the actual pelvic fin area under the body, circle 3 should be shifted slightly back to cover the eyes and forehead, and circle 1 should encompass more of the body.

marks.label: Pale sandy body
marks.description: Almost translucent, pale sandy-mottled - matches clean sand. Slightly larger and paler than the common goby.

marks.label: Pelvic-fin sucker
marks.description: Perches on the sand on the fused pelvic-fin sucker shared by all gobies.

marks.label: Rows of small saddles
marks.description: Faint rows of small dark saddles/spots along the back; a row of spots on the first dorsal in males.

perMark.label: Pale sandy body
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and overlaps the dorsal fin and background instead of focusing on the body coloration.

perMark.label: Pelvic-fin sucker
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the side of the body and pectoral fin, completely missing the pelvic-fin sucker underneath.

perMark.label: Rows of small saddles
perMark.note: The circle is too large and centered on the dorsal fin rather than the saddles along the back.

validation.summary: The circles are poorly placed and far too large, overlapping each other and missing the specific anatomical features. They need to be resized and moved to the correct locations on the fish.

marks.label: Solid domed bell
marks.description: A thick, firm, dome-shaped bell (the 'dustbin-lid' jelly) with a violet rim - much more solid than the floppy saucer jellies.

marks.label: Eight frilly arms, no tentacles
marks.description: Eight thick, cauliflower-frilly mouth-arms hang below, and there are NO long trailing tentacles. Mostly harmless.

perMark.label: Solid domed bell
perMark.note: The circle is shifted too far to the right, leaving half of it floating in the empty water.

perMark.label: Eight frilly arms, no tentacles
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far to the left, missing the main cauliflower-like frilly sections of the arms.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and very clear, but both circles are shifted too far outward into the open water. Move circle 1 leftward to center on the solid dome, and move circle 2 rightward to cover the thick frilly mouth-arms.

marks.label: Many small dark spots
marks.description: Densely peppered with small dark brown spots over a sandy-buff body — a small (to ~75 cm) shark, not a fish.

marks.label: Two dorsal fins set far back
marks.description: Both dorsal fins are set well back towards the tail, a shark-shape giveaway; the body is rough like sandpaper.

perMark.label: Many small dark spots
perMark.note: The circle is far too large and includes a massive amount of sandy background instead of focusing on the shark's skin.

perMark.label: Two dorsal fins set far back
perMark.note: The circle is positioned too far forward, capturing only the first dorsal fin and missing the second one further down the tail.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent, but the circles need to be resized and moved. Shrink circle 1 to focus tightly on the spotted skin, and shift circle 2 further down the tail to clearly encompass both dorsal fins.

marks.label: W-shaped pupil
marks.description: The eye has a distinctive wavy, W-shaped pupil - a cuttlefish signature you won't see on a fish.

marks.label: Fin runs the whole body
marks.description: A narrow fin skirts the entire length of the broad, flattened body on both sides (a squid's fins sit only at the tail).

marks.label: Broad mottled body
marks.description: Broad, flattened oval body, mottled brown and able to flash zebra patterns. Our largest cuttlefish; the white cuttlebone often washes up.

perMark.label: W-shaped pupil
perMark.note: The distinctive W-shaped pupil is perfectly centered and highly visible.

perMark.label: Fin runs the whole body
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the lower flank and captures the edge of the fin, but could be centered better on the fin itself.

perMark.label: Broad mottled body
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the upper back showing the mottled skin, but the label number 3 is swapped with label number 2.

validation.summary: The labels for marks 2 and 3 are swapped. Swap the numbers so that mark 2 points to the fin and mark 3 points to the broad mottled body.

marks.label: Small ear-like fins
marks.description: Two small, rounded ear-like fins near the head (not running down the body) mark out the little bobtail.

marks.label: Stubby body, iridescent specks
marks.description: A tiny (about 2 cm), stubby rounded body flecked with iridescent specks. Buries in clean sand by day with only the eyes showing.

perMark.label: Small ear-like fins
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the back of the head and mantle rather than on the actual ear-like fins.

perMark.label: Stubby body, iridescent specks
perMark.note: The circle perfectly highlights the stubby body covered in iridescent specks and chromatophores.

validation.summary: Circle 1 needs to be moved to the actual ear-like fin on the side of the mantle, while Circle 2 is well-placed on the iridescent body.

marks.label: Extremely long, thin body
marks.description: An exceptionally slender, elongated body tapering to a long pointed snout — quite unlike any other small UK fish.

marks.label: Long pointed snout
marks.description: A long, fine, tweezer-like snout for picking tiny crustaceans out of weed.

marks.label: Row of short spines before dorsal
marks.description: A row of about fifteen tiny separate spines runs along the back in front of the dorsal fin (hence the name); a slim tail.

perMark.label: Extremely long, thin body
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the mid-body but is too small to encompass the full elongated shape of the fish.

perMark.label: Long pointed snout
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the gill cover and eye area, completely missing the long snout.

perMark.label: Row of short spines before dorsal
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the mid-body flank where no dorsal spines are visible.

validation.summary: The photo is a good lateral shot of the species, but the circles are poorly placed. Circle 2 needs to be moved forward to cover the snout, and Circle 3 needs to be moved up to the dorsal ridge where the tiny spines are located.

marks.label: Squat rounded whorl
marks.description: A squat, bluntly conical shell with purple zigzag streaks - flatter and rounder than the pointed painted top shell.

marks.label: Open navel hole underneath
marks.description: The giveaway: a distinct open navel (umbilicus) hole at the centre of the underside. The painted top shell has none.

marks.label: Squat rounded shell
marks.description: A low, rounded top-shell whorl, not a tall pointed spire.

marks.label: Reddish-purple streaks
marks.description: Diagonal reddish-purple streaks over a grey-green shell; an open navel-hole (umbilicus) sits at the base.

perMark.label: Squat rounded whorl
perMark.note: The circle encompasses the shell whorl, but the shell is heavily worn and covered in green algae, obscuring the diagnostic purple zigzag streaks.

perMark.label: Open navel hole underneath
perMark.note: The shell is viewed from above, meaning the underside and the open navel hole are completely hidden from view.

perMark.label: Squat rounded shell
perMark.note: The circle is very small and placed off-center near the apex, which does not effectively show the overall squat shape of the shell.

perMark.label: Reddish-purple streaks
perMark.note: The shell is eroded and green with algae, lacking any visible reddish-purple streaks or the open navel at the base.

validation.summary: The current photo shows an eroded, algae-covered specimen from an overhead angle that completely hides the diagnostic open navel underneath. Replace this photo with one showing both the underside of the shell to reveal the umbilicus and a specimen with clear reddish-purple zigzag patterns.

marks.label: Bony scute ridge
marks.description: A row of hard, bony, keel-like scutes runs along the full length of the lateral line — unmistakable once you know to look.

marks.label: Two separate dorsal fins
marks.description: First dorsal is short and spiny; second is long and soft — two clearly separate fins unlike mackerel's continuous one.

marks.label: Lateral-line scutes
marks.description: A row of bony plates (scutes) along the lateral line - the key give-away of horse mackerel (a scad, family Carangidae).

marks.label: Large eye, blunt snout
marks.description: A notably large eye and blunt rounded head; metallic silver flanks without mackerel's wavy green bars.

marks.label: Large eye
marks.description: A noticeably large eye for the head - one of the first things you notice.

marks.label: Forked tail, slim body
marks.description: A slim, faintly striped silver body ending in a deeply forked tail.

perMark.label: Bony scute ridge
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and overlaps multiple areas, but it does encompass the scute ridge.

perMark.label: Two separate dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle is too large and shifted low, capturing the body and lateral line rather than focusing on the dorsal fins.

perMark.label: Lateral-line scutes
perMark.note: The circle is oversized and centered too low, though the lateral line is visible inside it.

perMark.label: Large eye, blunt snout
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the lower gill cover and throat area, completely missing the eye and snout.

perMark.label: Large eye
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the caudal peduncle near the tail, far away from the eye.

perMark.label: Forked tail, slim body
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the operculum behind the eye, completely missing the tail.

validation.summary: The marker circles are completely scrambled, with the eye and tail labels pointing to the wrong ends of the fish, and the body markers being far too large. Re-author all markers to correctly target their corresponding features.

marks.label: Chin barbel
marks.description: A single whisker under the chin (shared with cod and poor cod; absent in pollack and saithe).

marks.label: Dark pectoral spot
marks.description: A dark blotch at the base of the pectoral fin - strongest in bib, and often the first thing you notice.

marks.label: Deep coppery body
marks.description: Much deeper-bodied than pollack or saithe, coppery with faint vertical bars - almost a bream-like profile.

perMark.label: Chin barbel
perMark.note: The circle is placed below the chin on the sandy background, missing the actual barbel which is visible just to the right.

perMark.label: Dark pectoral spot
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far forward on the gill cover instead of the pectoral fin base.

perMark.label: Deep coppery body
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the deep body shape and vertical coppery bars.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent, but circles 1 and 2 are misaligned. Move circle 1 slightly up and right to cover the chin barbel, and move circle 2 back to the base of the pectoral fin.

marks.label: Chin barbel
marks.description: A short whisker under the chin, like bib's. Poor cod is the smaller, slimmer of the two.

marks.label: Large eye
marks.description: A noticeably large eye for the size of the head - poor cod is a small (to ~15 cm), big-eyed gadoid.

marks.label: Slimmer body than bib
marks.description: Coppery but more slender and tapering than the deep-bodied bib; three dorsal fins.

perMark.label: Chin barbel
perMark.note: The circle is placed too low in the dark background, completely missing the chin barbel.

perMark.label: Large eye
perMark.note: The circle is well-centered on the large, prominent eye.

perMark.label: Slimmer body than bib
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the slender, tapering mid-body profile.

validation.summary: The photo is of good quality, but circle 1 is misaligned and sits entirely in the dark space below the fish's chin. Reposition circle 1 upward to target the actual chin barbel.
